findings.title: Potential G704: SSRF via taint analysis
findings.description:
SSRF via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the targetURL parameter (e.g., via query string or form input)
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a request to the vulnerable endpoint with targetURL set to an internal IP (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/). 2. The handler at main.go:157 calls http.Get(targetURL) without validation. 3. The server makes an HTTP request to the attacker-controlled URL, potentially accessing internal services.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can probe internal network services, access cloud metadata endpoints (e.g., AWS IAM credentials), or perform port scanning of internal hosts, leading to lateral movement and data exfiltration.
attack_scenario.explanation: The code directly uses user-supplied input as a URL in http.Get() with no allowlist or validation, enabling SSRF. This is a real vulnerability.

findings.title: Potential G701: SQL injection via taint analysis
findings.description:
SQL injection via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
71 | 70: 	query := "SELECT id, username, role FROM users WHERE username='" + username + "' AND password='" + password + "'"
71: 	row := db.QueryRow(query)
72:

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can supply username and password parameters (e.g., via login form)
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker submits a login request with username = ' OR '1'='1' -- and any password. 2. The query at line 70 becomes: SELECT id, username, role FROM users WHERE username='' OR '1'='1' --' AND password='...'. 3. The SQL injection bypasses authentication, returning the first user (likely admin).
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker gains unauthorized access to any user account, including admin, and can retrieve all user data from the users table.
attack_scenario.explanation: String concatenation of user input into SQL query without parameterization is classic SQL injection. This is a real vulnerability.

findings.title: Potential G703: Path traversal via taint analysis
findings.description:
Path traversal via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the filename parameter (e.g., via query string or form input)
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a request with filename = '../../etc/passwd'. 2. The filepath at line 135 becomes './files/../../etc/passwd' which resolves to './etc/passwd' (or '/etc/passwd' depending on OS). 3. os.ReadFile reads the file and returns its contents.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can read arbitrary files on the server filesystem, such as /etc/passwd, application configuration files, or source code, leading to information disclosure and potential privilege escalation.
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is concatenated into a file path without sanitization, allowing directory traversal. This is a real vulnerability.

findings.title: Potential G702: Command injection via taint analysis
findings.description:
Command injection via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[Also reported by another step: Subprocess launched with variable
Gosec confidence: 90%]

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the cmd parameter (e.g., via query string or form input)
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a request with cmd = 'ls; cat /etc/shadow'. 2. The command at line 114 becomes: bash -c 'ls; cat /etc/shadow'. 3. The shell executes both commands, returning the output of the second command.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary system commands on the server, leading to full server compromise, data exfiltration, or lateral movement.
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is passed directly to bash -c without sanitization, enabling command injection. This is a real vulnerability.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the input parameter (e.g., via query string or form input)
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a request with input = '<script>alert(1)</script>'. 2. The handler at line 210 writes JSON with the unescaped input: {"algorithm":"md5","input":"<script>alert(1)</script>","hash":"..."}. 3. If the response is rendered in a browser (e.g., via a web page that fetches this API), the script executes.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser, leading to session hijacking, phishing, or defacement.
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is directly interpolated into a JSON response without escaping. Although Content-Type is application/json, if the response is embedded in HTML without proper encoding, XSS is possible. This is a real vulnerability.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — The response Content-Type is application/octet-stream, which forces a download. Browsers will not render the content as HTML, so XSS is not possible. This is a false positive.]

attack_scenario.explanation: The response Content-Type is application/octet-stream, which forces a download. Browsers will not render the content as HTML, so XSS is not possible. This is a false positive.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — The response Content-Type is text/plain, which browsers will not interpret as HTML. XSS is not possible. This is a false positive.]

attack_scenario.explanation: The response Content-Type is text/plain, which browsers will not interpret as HTML. XSS is not possible. This is a false positive.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the html parameter (e.g., via query string or form input)
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a request with html = '<script>alert(1)</script>'. 2. The handler at line 100 writes the raw HTML to the response with no Content-Type header (defaults to text/html). 3. The browser renders the script, executing it.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser, leading to session hijacking, phishing, or defacement.
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is written directly to the response without escaping and with no Content-Type set (defaults to text/html), enabling stored/reflected XSS. This is a real vulnerability.

findings.title: Potential G401: Use of weak cryptographic primitive
findings.description:
Use of weak cryptographic primitive
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
206 | 205: 	// VULNERABLE: MD5 is cryptographically broken
206: 	hash := md5.Sum([]byte(input))
207: 	hexHash := fmt.Sprintf("%x", hash)

attack_scenario.actor: attacker who can obtain the MD5 hash (e.g., via database breach or network sniffing)
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker obtains the MD5 hash of a password from the application (e.g., via SQL injection or data leak). 2. Attacker uses rainbow tables or brute-force to reverse the MD5 hash (MD5 is fast and collision-prone). 3. Attacker recovers the plaintext password.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can recover plaintext passwords, leading to account takeover. MD5 is cryptographically broken and should not be used for security-sensitive hashing.
attack_scenario.explanation: MD5 is a weak cryptographic primitive and should not be used for password hashing or integrity checks. This is a real vulnerability.

findings.title: Potential G114: Use of net/http serve function that has no support for setting timeouts
findings.description:
Use of net/http serve function that has no support for setting timeouts
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: remote attacker who can send requests to the server
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker opens many slow connections (e.g., Slowloris) to the server. 2. Because no timeouts are set, the server keeps connections open indefinitely. 3. Eventually, the server runs out of file descriptors or goroutines, causing denial of service.
attack_scenario.impact: Denial of service: the application becomes unresponsive to legitimate users.
attack_scenario.explanation: http.ListenAndServe without timeouts is vulnerable to slow-connection attacks. This is a real vulnerability, though severity is medium.

attack_scenario.path: Same as IRON-GOSEC-002: SQL injection via string concatenation.
attack_scenario.impact: Same as IRON-GOSEC-002: authentication bypass and data retrieval.
attack_scenario.explanation: Duplicate of IRON-GOSEC-002, but still a real vulnerability.

findings.title: Potential Generic API key in config
findings.description: Regex pattern detected a potential Generic API key in config at main.go:31. This may be a hardcoded secret that automated scanners missed.
findings.code_snippet: 31 | const apiKey = "sk-abc123def456ghi789jkl012mno345pqr678stu"

attack_scenario.actor: Any attacker with access to the source code repository or compiled binary (e.g., via public repo, insider, or decompilation).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker obtains the source code (e.g., clones public repo or extracts binary). 2. Reads line 31 of main.go: const apiKey = "sk-abc123def456ghi789jkl012mno345pqr678stu". 3. Uses the key to authenticate against the API service it belongs to (e.g., OpenAI, Stripe, etc.).
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker gains unauthorized access to the API service, potentially incurring costs, exfiltrating data, or performing actions on behalf of the application. The key appears to be a real API key (format matches common providers).
attack_scenario.explanation: The finding is a hardcoded API key in source code. The key format 'sk-...' is typical for secret keys (e.g., OpenAI). This is a real secret that should not be in code. The confidence is high because the pattern is unambiguous and the key is a constant string.

findings.title: SBOM generated: 1 components detected
findings.description: Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) generated in CycloneDX format. 1 components inventoried for vulnerability tracking.

findings.title: Not a git repository — supply chain checks limited
findings.description: Supply chain security checks (SBOM provenance, SLSA) require a git repository. Without git history, we cannot verify commit signatures or build provenance.

findings.title: SBOM generation available via syft
findings.description: Run 'syft scan <target> -o cyclonedx-json' to generate a Software Bill of Materials. SBOMs are required for supply chain transparency (US Executive Order 14028).
findings.fix_suggestion: syft scan battle_test_candidates/go_target -o cyclonedx-json > sbom.cdx.json

findings.title: OpenSSF Scorecard not installed
findings.description: Install OpenSSF Scorecard (https://github.com/ossf/scorecard) to get automated security health checks for your dependencies and build pipeline.
findings.fix_suggestion: go install github.com/ossf/scorecard/v5@latest

findings.title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) in proxy handler
findings.description: The handleProxy function takes a user-supplied 'url' query parameter and passes it directly to http.Get without any validation or allowlist. An attacker can make the server send requests to internal services (e.g., 169.254.169.254 for cloud metadata, internal APIs, or localhost) or external systems, potentially leading to information disclosure or further attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement a strict allowlist of permitted URLs or URL patterns. Validate that the target URL matches an allowed scheme (e.g., https only) and domain. Consider using a URL parser to reject internal IPs or private address ranges. Alternatively, avoid proxying arbitrary URLs altogether.

findings.title: OS Command Injection in handleExec handler
findings.description: The handleExec function takes a 'cmd' query parameter from the user and passes it directly to exec.Command with 'bash -c', allowing arbitrary command execution. No authentication, validation, or sanitization is performed on the input.
findings.fix_suggestion: Avoid using shell commands with user input. If command execution is required, use a whitelist of allowed commands and arguments, or use exec.Command with separate arguments instead of 'bash -c'. Consider removing this endpoint entirely if not needed.

findings.title: Path Traversal in handleFiles
findings.description: The 'filename' parameter from the URL query is concatenated into a file path without validation, allowing an attacker to read arbitrary files via path traversal (e.g., '../etc/passwd').
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate the filename to ensure it does not contain path traversal sequences (e.g., '..' or '/') and restrict it to a safe set of characters. Use filepath.Base() to strip directory components, or maintain a whitelist of allowed filenames.

findings.title: Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) in handleSearch
findings.description: User input from the 'query' query parameter is directly concatenated into an HTML string and written to the response without any escaping or sanitization. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by crafting a malicious query parameter, leading to reflected XSS.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use Go's html/template package to auto-escape user input, or manually escape the query value with html.EscapeString() before embedding it in the HTML.

findings.title: SQL Injection in handleLogin
findings.description: User-controlled input from HTTP form values 'username' and 'password' is directly concatenated into a SQL query string without any sanitization or parameterization. This allows an attacker to inject arbitrary SQL commands, potentially leading to authentication bypass, data exfiltration, or database compromise.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use parameterized queries (prepared statements) instead of string concatenation. For example: row := db.QueryRow("SELECT id, username, role FROM users WHERE username=? AND password=?", username, password)

findings.title: Missing authentication on proxy endpoint
findings.description: handleProxy allows anyone to make arbitrary HTTP requests without any authentication. This can be used to access internal services, exfiltrate data, or perform SSRF attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add authentication middleware (e.g., session token, API key) before processing the request. Also consider restricting access to trusted users only.

findings.title: Missing input validation on URL parameter
findings.description: The 'url' parameter is taken directly from user input and used in an HTTP request without any validation or allowlist. This enables SSRF attacks to internal networks and arbitrary external hosts.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate the URL against an allowlist of permitted hosts/schemes. Reject URLs pointing to private IP ranges, localhost, or unexpected schemes.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on proxy endpoint
findings.description: No rate limiting is applied to this endpoint, allowing an attacker to abuse it for large-scale SSRF scanning or data exfiltration.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting per IP or per user to restrict the number of requests within a time window.

findings.title: Missing authentication on command execution endpoint
findings.description: handleExec executes arbitrary shell commands without any authentication check. This allows unauthenticated remote code execution.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add authentication middleware (e.g., session token, API key, or JWT validation) before executing commands. Consider removing this endpoint entirely or restricting to admin-only with strong auth.

findings.title: Missing input validation on command parameter
findings.description: The 'cmd' parameter is taken directly from the URL query and passed to exec.Command without any validation, sanitization, or allowlisting. This enables arbitrary command injection.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate the 'cmd' parameter against a strict allowlist of safe commands and arguments. Never pass user input directly to shell execution. Consider using a safer API or removing this functionality.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on command execution endpoint
findings.description: No rate limiting is applied to this endpoint, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands repeatedly, potentially causing resource exhaustion or abuse.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting (e.g., per IP or per user) to restrict the number of requests to this endpoint within a time window.

findings.title: Missing authentication on file access endpoint
findings.description: handleFiles allows reading arbitrary files without any authentication check. An attacker can enumerate and read any file in the ./files/ directory.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add authentication middleware or check session token at the start of the function. For example: if !isAuthenticated(r) { http.Error(w, "Unauthorized", http.StatusUnauthorized); return }

findings.title: Missing input validation on filename parameter
findings.description: The filename parameter is used directly in a file path without any validation. This allows path traversal attacks (e.g., '../../etc/passwd') to read arbitrary files outside the intended directory.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate the filename: ensure it does not contain path separators or '..', use filepath.Base() to strip directory components, and consider using a whitelist of allowed filenames.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on file access endpoint
findings.description: No rate limiting is applied to this endpoint, allowing an attacker to perform rapid file enumeration or denial-of-service attacks by requesting many files in quick succession.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting middleware, e.g., using a token bucket or sliding window algorithm, to limit requests per IP or per user.

findings.title: Missing authentication on search endpoint
findings.description: handleSearch returns search results without any authentication check. While not as critical as admin endpoints, search functionality can leak sensitive data and should be protected.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add auth middleware or check session token at function start.

findings.title: Missing input validation on search query
findings.description: The 'query' parameter is taken directly from URL query string and reflected into HTML without any validation or sanitization, leading to reflected XSS.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate and sanitize user input. Use html.EscapeString() or a proper template engine to escape output.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on search endpoint
findings.description: No rate limiting is applied to the search endpoint, allowing an attacker to perform automated queries or brute-force attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting middleware, e.g., using token bucket or sliding window algorithm.

findings.title: Missing authentication on hash endpoint
findings.description: handleHash performs cryptographic hashing without any authentication check. While not accessing user data directly, it exposes a hash function that could be abused for hash computation or as part of a larger attack chain.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add authentication middleware or token validation to restrict access to authorized users only.

findings.title: Missing input validation on hash input parameter
findings.description: The 'input' query parameter is used directly in MD5 hashing and reflected in the JSON response without any validation or sanitization. This could lead to injection attacks or unexpected behavior if the input contains special characters.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate input length, character set, and format. Consider using a whitelist of allowed characters or limiting input size.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on hash endpoint
findings.description: The hash endpoint has no rate limiting, allowing an attacker to perform unlimited hash computations, potentially leading to resource exhaustion or abuse for password cracking attempts.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting per IP or per user to restrict the number of requests within a time window.

findings.title: Missing input validation on username and password
findings.description: The handler does not validate the format, length, or content of username and password before using them in a SQL query. This allows SQL injection attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate that username and password are non-empty strings with reasonable length limits. Use parameterized queries to safely handle input.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on login endpoint
findings.description: The login endpoint does not implement rate limiting, allowing brute-force attacks on user credentials.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting per IP address or per username. Consider using a middleware like 'github.com/didip/tollbooth' or a custom solution.

findings.title: Missing CSRF protection on login endpoint
findings.description: The login endpoint accepts POST requests without CSRF protection, making it vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement CSRF tokens for state-changing requests. Use a library like 'github.com/gorilla/csrf' or generate and validate tokens manually.

findings.title: Missing Content-Type validation
findings.description: The handler does not validate the Content-Type header of incoming requests. It expects form-encoded data but does not enforce it.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate that the Content-Type header is 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded' for form submissions.

findings.title: Missing authentication on admin user list endpoint
findings.description: handleAdminUsers returns all user data without any authentication check. Anyone can access this endpoint and enumerate all users.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add auth middleware. Example: wrap handler with RequireAuth() or check session token at function start.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on admin user list endpoint
findings.description: This endpoint returns sensitive user data and could be abused for enumeration or DoS. Rate limiting is recommended to mitigate automated scraping.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add rate limiting middleware. Example: use a token bucket or sliding window limiter per IP.

findings.title: Server-Side Request Forgery via user-controlled URL
findings.description: The function takes a 'url' parameter from user input and makes an HTTP GET request to that URL without any allowlist or validation, allowing an attacker to make requests to internal services.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement an allowlist of permitted URLs or domains, or validate the URL against a strict pattern.

findings.title: Command Injection via user-controlled 'cmd' parameter
findings.description: The function passes user input directly to 'bash -c' without sanitization, allowing arbitrary command execution.
findings.fix_suggestion: Avoid shell commands with user input. Use safer alternatives like exec.Command with separate arguments, or validate input against a whitelist.

findings.title: Path Traversal via user-controlled filename
findings.description: The function concatenates user input directly into a file path without validation, allowing access to arbitrary files outside the intended directory.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate the filename to prevent directory traversal (e.g., reject '..' or use filepath.Clean and ensure it stays within the base directory).

findings.title: Cross-Site Scripting via reflected user input
findings.description: User input is directly concatenated into HTML without escaping, allowing injection of arbitrary JavaScript.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use proper HTML escaping (e.g., html.EscapeString) or a template engine that auto-escapes.

findings.title: Use of broken MD5 hash algorithm
findings.description: MD5 is cryptographically broken and should not be used for security-sensitive operations.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use a strong hash function like SHA-256 or SHA-3.

findings.title: SQL Injection via string concatenation in login query
findings.description: User-supplied username and password are directly concatenated into a SQL query, allowing injection of arbitrary SQL.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use parameterized queries (prepared statements) instead of string concatenation.

findings.title: Missing authentication on admin endpoint
findings.description: The /api/admin/users endpoint exposes all user data without any authentication or authorization check.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement authentication (e.g., session token, JWT) and authorization (e.g., admin role check) before returning sensitive data.

findings.title: Server listens on all interfaces without TLS
findings.description: The server listens on port 8080 without TLS encryption, which may expose traffic to interception.
findings.code_snippet:
func main() {
	var err error
	db, err = sql.Open("sqlite3", "./app.db?"+dbPassword)
	if err != nil {
		log.Fatal(err)
	}
	defer db.Close()

	http.HandleFunc("/api/login", handleLogin)           // GT-003: SQL injection
	http.HandleFunc("/api/search", handleSearch)          // GT-004: XSS
	http.HandleFunc("/api/exec", handleExec)              // GT-005: Command injection
	http.HandleFunc("/api/files", handleFiles)            // GT-006: Path traversal
	http.HandleFunc("/api/proxy", handleProxy)            // GT-007: SSRF
	http.HandleFunc("/api/admin/users", handleAdminUsers) // GT-008: Missing auth
	http.HandleFunc("/api/hash", handleHash)              // GT-009: Weak crypto (MD5)
	http.HandleFunc("/api/health", handleHealth)          // safe endpoint (control)
	http.HandleFunc("/", handleIndex)                     // safe endpoint (control)

	log.Println("Vulnerable app listening on :8080")
	log.Fatal(http.ListenAndServe(":8080", nil))
}
findings.fix_suggestion: Use HTTPS with a valid certificate in production. Consider binding to localhost if external access is not needed.
